title: Lesson 2 Overview
subtitle: Listen to the lesson explained in English

transcript.en: Welcome to lesson two, Luis. This one is packed but practical. We'll cover a robbery dialogue with descriptive vocabulary, the colours, common adjectives, numbers up to thirty, and the months of the year.
which means 'I was robbed'. An elderly woman calls for help:

Somebody stole my bag. The passer-by asks if she saw who it was. To describe the thief in Maltese, you'll need adjectives that change form depending on whether the person is male or female. A man who is tall is
a woman who is tall is
Notice the -a ending for feminine. Same pattern for short:
for a woman. And for the police, the emergency number in Malta is one-one-two —
Now, colours. Most Maltese colours have three forms: masculine, feminine, and plural. Take white:
plural. The colour matches the gender and number of whatever you're describing. So a red van is
Some colours don't change — like blue,
stay the same in all three forms. The interactive section will walk you through all twelve colours one by one.
Next, adjectives in opposite pairs. Big is
Each adjective has masculine, feminine, and plural forms, just like the colours. Beautiful is
There are eleven pairs in total in the adjectives section.
Now numbers. From one to ten:
From eleven to twenty: ħdax, tnax, tlettax, erbatax, ħmistax, sittax, sbatax, tmintax, dsatax, and
twenty. From twenty-one onward you say the unit, then the word u meaning 'and', then twenty. So twenty-one is
A grammar tip: when the number is between two and ten, the noun that follows goes plural. So three years is
But from eleven onwards, the noun stays singular. So twenty-three years is

Lastly, months of the year, mostly inherited from Italian: Jannar, Frar, Marzu, April, Mejju, Ġunju, Lulju, Awwissu, Settembru, Ottubru, Novembru, and Diċembru. They'll click quickly because they sound similar to English.
That's the lesson. Pick the section you want to drill — colours, adjectives, numbers, or months — and the app will play each word for you. Saħħa.
